name: Gil Fuller
abstract: Walter Gilbert "Gil" Fuller (April 14, 1920, Los Angeles, California – May 26, 1994, San Diego, California) was an American jazz arranger. He is no relation to the jazz trumpeter and vocalist Walter "Rosetta" Fuller.

about_quotes.speaker: Joe Segal
about_quotes.evidence: [INTERVIEWER] ...was it Gil Fuller - then kind of put the finishing touches on it. ... [INTERVIEWEE] Right.
about_quotes.doc_title: Joe Segal — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Owens
about_quotes.evidence: Gil Fuller had called me and said, 'Jimmy, will you put together a big band for this tour?' ... Steve had backed out at the airport when we were leaving. He was afraid to fly and he got into an argument with Gil Fuller so he could say, 'Man, I quit!'
about_quotes.doc_title: Jimmy Owens — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Gerald Wilson
about_quotes.evidence: Then on the second one – the second volume, the second volume, Gil Fuller did two numbers, and he had Benny Carter do two numbers. I did the other four – I did four numbers. That’s on the second volume.
about_quotes.doc_title: Gerald Wilson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Holman Bill
about_quotes.evidence: Gil Fuller one time told me that he would have liked to have heard what would have happened if I had come up through a different band than Kenton
about_quotes.doc_title: Holman Bill — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Mario Bauza
about_quotes.evidence: [INTERVIEWER] ... Gil Fuller (phonetic), I think, also. And you have the time when you were from about "Afro-Jazz Suite"...

about_quotes.speaker: JJ Johnson
about_quotes.evidence: They were always well-arranged by persons such as Gil Fuller or Tadd Dameron and people of that ilk.
about_quotes.doc_title: JJ Johnson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
